section.title: Do Not Love the World
section.sectionRationale: Having tested love for God by obedience and love for the brothers, John names love's great rival: the world. He warns that love for the world and love for the Father cannot coexist, exposes the world's cravings, and sets the passing world against those who do God's will and remain forever.
section.thesis: Because the world and its desires are passing away and stand opposed to the Father, believers must not give their love to the world, for love of the world crowds out love for the Father, while whoever does the will of God remains forever.
section.frameNote: Three beats: the command not to love the world; what the world offers, the cravings of flesh, eyes, and pride; and the contrast of the passing world with the one who does God's will.

units.label: Do not love the world
units.note: two loves that cannot share a heart

verses.text: Do not {{agapao|love}} the {{kosmos|world}} or anything in the world. If anyone loves the world, the love of the Father is not in him.

units.label: All that is in the world
units.note: the cravings that are not from the Father

verses.text: For all that is in the world, the {{epithymia|desires}} of the flesh, the desires of the eyes, and the {{alazoneia|pride}} of life, is not from the Father but from the world.

units.label: The world is passing away
units.note: but the one who does God's will remains

verses.text: The world is {{parago|passing away}}, along with its desires; but whoever does the {{thelema|will}} of God {{meno|remains}} forever.

agapao.gloss: To love, to set one's affection and devotion on something. It is the same strong verb used for love of God and neighbour. Here it is misdirected: to love the world in this way is to give it the devotion owed to the Father, and the two cannot share the same heart.

kosmos.gloss: World, order, system. In John it often means not the created earth or people as objects of God's love, but the whole system of human life organised in rebellion against God. It is this God-opposing order, with its values and cravings, that believers must not love.

epithymia.gloss: Desire, craving, longing, often for what is forbidden. The desires of the flesh and of the eyes are disordered appetites, wanting and coveting apart from God. The word is neutral in itself but here names the sinful cravings that the world stirs up and feeds.

alazoneia.gloss: Pride, boastful pretension, the arrogance of self-display. The pride of life is the boasting in what one has and does, the swagger of self-sufficiency. It is the opposite of humble dependence on the Father and belongs to the world, not to Him.

parago.gloss: To pass away, pass by, be in the process of vanishing. The present tense pictures the world already passing off the scene like a receding tide. To love the world is to bind one's heart to what is even now slipping away.

thelema.gloss: Will, desire, purpose. The will of God is what He wills and commands, and doing it marks those who belong to Him rather than to the world. Set against the fleeting cravings of the world, God's will is the path of what endures.

meno.gloss: To remain, abide, continue, endure. The same key Johannine word for abiding in Christ. Whoever does the will of God remains forever, standing in sharp contrast to the world and its desires that are passing away. What is done in line with God's will has permanence.

basic.context: John has been testing true faith by love: love for God shown in obedience, and love for fellow believers. Now he warns about a rival love. Do not love the world, he says, meaning the whole way of life organised against God, with its cravings and pride. You cannot give your heart to that and to the Father at once. And there is a clock on it: the world is passing away, but those who do God's will last forever.
basic.hermeneutics: The world here is not the planet or people, whom God loves, but the God-opposing system and its values. John is not calling for a joyless retreat from creation or culture, but for undivided love toward the Father. The test is where our deepest devotion lies, seen in what we crave and boast in, and it is framed by hope: only what aligns with God's will endures.
basic.originalLanguages: Do not love, agapao, the world, kosmos, the whole order set against God. Its offer is the desires, epithymia, of flesh and eyes and the pride, alazoneia, of life. All of it is passing away, parago, even now, but whoever does the will, thelema, of God remains, meno, forever.
basic.typology: The two loves recall the covenant call to love the LORD with all the heart and to have no other gods. The passing world set against the enduring will of God echoes the prophets' contrast between the fading nations and the kingdom that will not pass away.
basic.christ: Jesus overcame the world and its temptations, refusing the very cravings and pride John names, and He calls His people to follow. In Him the will of God is done perfectly, and He is the one in whom those who do God's will remain forever.
basic.god: The Father is the rightful owner of our love, and He is jealous for it, warning that love of the world drives out love for Him. He is also the one whose will endures when the world fades, so that to align with Him is to share in what lasts forever.
basic.crossReferences: Matthew 6:24 says no one can serve two masters. James 4:4 calls friendship with the world enmity with God. Matthew 4 shows Jesus refusing the world's cravings and pride in the wilderness.
basic.oneStory: From the first temptation, the world has offered what is pleasing to the eye and promising to make us wise apart from God. John exposes that same lure and sets against it the will of God, done perfectly in Christ, which alone endures. To love the Father over the passing world is to belong to the story that does not end.

context.literary: A compact, three-verse unit built on sharp contrasts: love of the world against love of the Father, the cravings of the world against what is from the Father, and the passing world against the one who remains forever. The threefold description in verse 16 is almost proverbial in its rhythm.
context.historical: The readers lived in a culture saturated with idolatry, consumption, and status-seeking, and the false teachers may have made peace with these worldly values. John calls the church to a rival allegiance, warning that the surrounding order is under judgment and passing away.
context.canonical: The passage joins the consistent biblical warning against divided loyalty, Matthew 6:24, James 4:4, and against the idolatry of the nations, and it echoes the temptation narratives where Jesus refuses the world's offers, setting the pattern His people follow.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To warn believers that love for the world and love for the Father cannot coexist, to expose the world's offer as craving and pride that come from the world rather than from God, and to anchor them in the hope that doing God's will leads to what endures forever.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Verse 15 is a direct command, do not love the world, and verses 16 and 17 give the reasons: the nature of the world's offer and its passing away. The abiding principle is undivided love for the Father and a life shaped by His enduring will.

debates.question: What is 'the world' that believers must not love, given that God loves the world in John 3:16?

views.label: The God-opposing system of values, not creation or people
views.case: John uses 'world' in several senses; here the world is the whole order of human life organised against God, marked by the cravings of flesh and eyes and the pride of life. This is distinct from the created order God made good and the people God loves and sends His Son to save. This reading harmonises John 3:16 with 1 John 2:15 and fits the description in verse 16.

views.label: The physical creation itself, to be despised
views.case: Some have read this as a call to reject the material world as evil; but that contradicts the goodness of creation and God's love for the world in John 3:16.

views.label: Simply other people, to be kept at arm's length
views.case: Others reduce it to avoiding non-Christians; but John elsewhere sends believers to love and witness to the world God loves.

debates.bearing: The strongest orthodox reading takes 'the world' as the sinful system opposed to God, its cravings and pride, not the good creation or the people God loves. Believers are to enjoy God's gifts and love their neighbours while refusing to give their hearts to the world's godless order.

study.originalLanguages: The command is blunt: do not agapao, do not love, the kosmos, the world understood as the whole order of life set against God. Its whole offer is summed up in three cravings: the epithymia, the desires, of the flesh and of the eyes, and the alazoneia, the proud, boastful self-display John calls the pride of life. None of it comes from the Father. And it carries the seed of its own end, for the world is parago, passing away, even now slipping off the scene, while the one who does the thelema, the will of God, will meno, remain, abide forever. The same verb used for abiding in Christ marks the permanence of a life lived toward God.

typology.type: The two rival loves, the world and the Father
typology.fulfillment: The covenant call to love the LORD with the whole heart and have no other gods, brought to its point in Jesus who served God alone and refused the world's offers, Matthew 4:1-11.

typology.type: The passing world set against what endures
typology.fulfillment: The prophetic contrast between the fading nations and the kingdom that will not pass away, fulfilled in Christ and the eternal life He gives to those who do God's will.

study.christ: Jesus faced the very offers John names, the craving for bread, the spectacle before the eyes, and the pride of power, and He refused them all, loving the Father with an undivided heart. He overcame the world, and in Him the will of God is done perfectly. He is the one in whom those who do God's will remain forever, so that resisting the world's pull is not grim self-denial but following the Lord who has already conquered.
study.god: The Father rightly claims our whole love and warns that it cannot be shared with the world. He is not a rival to our joy but its true home, for His will is the one thing that endures when the world and its desires fade. To align our lives with Him is to be caught up into what lasts forever, which shows a God who calls us away from passing things for our lasting good.

crossReferences.note: No one can serve two masters; you cannot serve both God and money, the same undivided loyalty John presses.
Friendship with the world is enmity with God, echoing that love of the world crowds out love for the Father.
Jesus refuses the cravings and pride the world offers, the pattern His people follow in not loving the world.
Do not be conformed to this world but be transformed, and so discern and do the will of God that endures.

study.oneStory: Since the garden the world has dangled what looks good to the eye and promises life apart from God. John names that ancient lure, craving and pride, and sets against it the will of God, done perfectly by Jesus who refused every offer and overcame the world. The world is already passing away; only what is done in line with God's will remains forever. To love the Father above the fading world is to belong to the one story that does not end.

sermon.title: The Love of the World (Part 2)
sermon.why: Keller preaches this passage, 1 John 2:15-17, on what it means to love the world, the cravings and pride it offers, and the freedom of loving the Father whose will endures forever. A clear, gospel-centred handling of the text.

quiz.q: What does John mean by 'the world' that believers must not love?

options.text: The whole system of life organised against God, with its cravings and pride.
options.note: Verse 16 defines it by desire and pride; it is the God-opposing order, not creation or people.

options.text: Other people in general, to be kept at a distance.
options.note: It is not people as such, whom God loves; it is the godless system and its values.

options.text: The physical creation, which is evil and to be despised.
options.note: Creation is good; John targets the sinful order, not the material world itself.

options.text: Anything enjoyable, since pleasure is sinful.
options.note: John does not condemn enjoyment of God's gifts but love given to the godless world.

quiz.q: According to verse 16, where do the world's cravings and pride come from?

options.text: From the world, not from the Father.
options.note: John says plainly they are not from the Father but from the world.

options.text: From the Father, as good gifts to be pursued.
options.note: The opposite; these cravings are expressly not from the Father.

options.text: From a neutral source with no spiritual significance.
options.note: John treats them as spiritually significant, marking the world against the Father.

options.text: Partly from God and partly from the world.
options.note: John draws a clean line; they are from the world, not a mixture.

quiz.q: Why is loving the world so foolish, according to verse 17?

options.text: Because the world is passing away, while the one who does God's will remains forever.
options.note: To love the world is to bind your heart to what is already vanishing.

options.text: Because the world will always be here, so it is boring.
options.note: The point is the opposite: the world is passing away, not permanent.

options.text: Because enjoying anything is always a distraction.
options.note: The issue is misplaced love, not enjoyment as such.

options.text: Because the world sometimes disappoints us.
options.note: Deeper than disappointment: the world is passing away entirely, while God's will endures.

quiz.q: What does John say cannot coexist in a person's heart?

options.text: Love for the world and the love of the Father.
options.note: Verse 15: if anyone loves the world, the love of the Father is not in him.

options.text: Enjoyment of good gifts and gratitude to God.
options.note: These can and should coexist; the clash is love of the godless world versus love for the Father.
This is not John's contrast at all.

options.text: Friendship with unbelievers and faithfulness to God.
options.note: Loving people is right; it is devotion to the world's godless order that clashes with love for the Father.

questions: John says love for the world and love for the Father cannot share a heart. What in the world's offer competes most strongly for your devotion right now?
The world markets itself through the cravings of the flesh, the cravings of the eyes, and the pride of life. Which of these three do you find most tempting, and where do you see it at work?
The world is passing away. How would treating the world as a receding tide rather than solid ground change the way you spend your time and money?
Whoever does the will of God remains forever. What is one part of God's will you can give yourself to this week that will still matter in eternity?
Jesus refused the world's cravings and pride and calls us to follow. How does knowing that He already overcame the world help you resist its pull?

translationNotes.note: 'The world' here translates kosmos in the sense of the God-opposing order of life, distinct from the created world and the people God loves in John 3:16; context fixes the sense.
'The pride of life' renders alazoneia, boastful pretension over one's possessions and status; the phrase describes arrogant self-display, not simply having possessions.
'Is passing away' translates a present tense picturing the world already in the act of vanishing, set against 'remains forever', the same verb John uses for abiding in Christ.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 1 John 2:15-17. The two em dashes in verse 16 ('the world, the desires of the flesh ... the pride of life, is not from the Father') are rendered as commas per Makor style.
